sections.plain_summary: Methane SIBO (now more precisely called Intestinal Methanogen Overgrowth, or IMO) occurs when methane-producing archaea — primarily Methanobrevibacter smithii — are present in excessive numbers in the gut. Unlike bacterial SIBO, these are not bacteria but a separate class of microorganism. Methane gas slows the movement of the intestine, which is why the hallmark symptom is constipation rather than diarrhea. Many people with this condition have been told they have IBS-C or chronic constipation with no clear cause, and remain undiagnosed for years.
sections.detailed_summary:
Methane SIBO — more accurately termed Intestinal Methanogen Overgrowth (IMO) — is a condition in which archaea, primarily Methanobrevibacter smithii, overpopulate the gut and produce excessive methane gas. Unlike hydrogen-dominant SIBO, which is driven by bacteria and typically causes diarrhea or mixed-pattern symptoms, methane overgrowth is strongly associated with constipation. Methane gas itself has been shown in animal and human research to slow intestinal transit time — it acts on smooth muscle in a way that reduces peristaltic activity. This makes the condition mechanistically distinct, not just a variant of other SIBO types.

Because methanogens reside throughout the gut — not exclusively in the small intestine — the term 'IMO' was introduced by some researchers to better reflect the biology. The distinction matters clinically: the methanogen overgrowth may be present in the colon as well as the small intestine, which may explain why some treatment protocols targeting only the small bowel produce incomplete results.

Diagnosis is typically made via a lactulose or glucose breath test measuring both hydrogen and methane. Elevated methane (generally ≥10 ppm at any point during the test, though criteria vary by lab) is the key finding. Treatment often combines rifaximin with neomycin or metronidazole — the addition of a second antibiotic targeting methanogens is more effective than rifaximin alone. Herbal antimicrobials such as allicin (derived from garlic) are also used, particularly by integrative practitioners.

Many people with this condition have spent years being told they have idiopathic constipation or IBS-C, receiving laxatives and fiber recommendations that provide minimal or inconsistent relief. Self-directed investigation via breath testing — now available through some direct-to-consumer labs — has helped many people get to this diagnosis without relying on a gastroenterologist who may not test for methane.

prevalence.summary: A meaningful minority of people with IBS-C or chronic constipation — estimates suggest methane-positive breath tests occur in roughly one-third of people investigated for functional constipation.
prevalence.estimate: Methane-positive breath tests are found in approximately 30–50% of people with IBS-C, and in 10–15% of general IBS populations tested; population-level prevalence of symptomatic IMO is not well established.
prevalence.notes: Prevalence figures vary considerably depending on the testing protocol, cutoff thresholds used, and the population studied. Some labs use a cutoff of ≥10 ppm methane at any point in the test; others use different thresholds. IMO may be underdiagnosed because methane is not measured in all breath test panels — older tests measured only hydrogen. Methane elevation appears more common in women and in people with a history of prolonged constipation.

subgroups.name: IMO with pure constipation (IBS-C overlap)
subgroups.description: The most common presentation: slow transit constipation driven by methane-related reduction in gut motility. Often misdiagnosed as functional constipation or IBS-C.
distinguishing_features: Predominantly or exclusively constipated — few or no loose stools
Significant abdominal bloating that worsens throughout the day
Breath test shows elevated methane with low or absent hydrogen

subgroups.name: Mixed hydrogen + methane SIBO
subgroups.description: Some individuals produce both hydrogen and methane, suggesting concurrent bacterial SIBO alongside methanogen overgrowth. Symptoms may be more variable — alternating constipation and diarrhea.
distinguishing_features: Both hydrogen and methane elevated on breath test
May alternate between constipation and looser stools
Often associated with more complex dysbiosis or underlying motility issues
May require broader treatment targeting both hydrogen producers and methanogens

subgroups.name: Post-infectious IMO
subgroups.description: Methanogen overgrowth that develops following a gut infection (e.g. gastroenteritis), possibly via disruption of the migrating motor complex or changes in the microbial environment that favor methanogens.
distinguishing_features: Clear onset following a gastrointestinal illness
May be associated with post-infectious IBS pattern
Constipation symptoms that began after an acute infection
May resolve more readily with targeted treatment if the underlying motility disruption is addressed

subgroups.name: IMO secondary to structural or motility disorder
subgroups.description: Methanogen overgrowth driven by an underlying cause — such as gastroparesis, adhesions, hypothyroidism, or dysmotility — that slows intestinal transit and creates favorable conditions for methanogens.
distinguishing_features: Associated history of abdominal surgery, diabetes, thyroid disease, or neurological disorder
Investigation of root cause is essential for durable improvement

symptoms.notes: The most characteristic symptom — often severe, with infrequent bowel movements (fewer than 3 per week), hard or pellet-like stools, and significant straining. Frequently unresponsive to fiber or over-the-counter laxatives.
Typically worsens throughout the day and after meals. Many people describe a visible distension by evening. May be present even after a bowel movement.
A persistent feeling that the bowel has not fully emptied, which can cause repeated attempts at defecation.
Often dull or pressure-like rather than sharp; may be diffuse or concentrated in the lower abdomen.
Gas may be odourless or have a distinct smell depending on co-occurring organisms. Volume is often greater than peers eating the same diet.

symptoms.symptom: Slow gastric emptying / feeling of fullness
symptoms.notes: Methane-related dysmotility can affect the upper GI tract as well, producing early satiety or prolonged fullness after meals.
More common when methane overgrowth co-exists with gastroparesis or upper GI dysmotility.
May reflect impaired nutrient absorption, systemic inflammation, or the metabolic burden of chronic dysbiosis. Often disproportionate to the GI symptoms.
Difficulty concentrating, word-finding issues, or mental sluggishness reported by a subset of people with IMO. Mechanism is not fully understood but may involve gut-brain axis signalling.

symptoms.symptom: Weight gain or difficulty losing weight
symptoms.notes: Methanogens increase caloric extraction from fermented carbohydrates; research has linked elevated methane production with higher BMI and increased energy harvest from food.
High-fibre and fermentable carbohydrate (FODMAP) foods often worsen symptoms because they provide substrate for methanogens.

key_patterns: Methane ≥10 ppm at any point during a lactulose or glucose breath test (note: some labs and researchers use different thresholds)
Constipation-dominant pattern that is unexplained by structural causes on imaging or colonoscopy
Symptoms worsening on high-fibre or FODMAP-rich diets
Prior normal colonoscopy and stool tests with no structural explanation for constipation
Partial or temporary response to antibiotics, followed by relapse — suggesting ongoing overgrowth

at_home.name: Symptom and food journal
at_home.description: Tracking bowel frequency, stool consistency (using the Bristol Stool Scale), bloating severity, and foods consumed can help identify patterns and establish a baseline before testing. A structured 2–4 week journal is more useful than unstructured observation.
at_home.safety_notes: No safety concerns. Useful to bring to appointments with any practitioner.

at_home.name: At-home breath test (direct-to-consumer)
at_home.description: Several companies (e.g. Trio-Smart, AIRE, some integrative lab services) offer postal breath test kits that measure hydrogen and methane following a lactulose or glucose challenge. The individual collects breath samples at timed intervals at home and returns the kit for analysis.
at_home.safety_notes: The preparatory diet (usually 24 hours of low-fermentable foods) must be followed carefully — not doing so can invalidate results. Those with diabetes should note that the lactulose challenge involves a specific substrate. Results should ideally be interpreted with a knowledgeable practitioner rather than in isolation.

clinical.name: Lactulose breath test (hydrogen and methane)
clinical.description: The most widely used diagnostic test. The person drinks a lactulose solution (a non-absorbable sugar) after an overnight fast, then breathes into collection tubes at regular intervals (typically every 20 minutes for 2–3 hours). Levels of hydrogen and methane are measured. A methane reading of ≥10 ppm at any point is commonly used to indicate IMO, though criteria vary. Lactulose tests the entire small intestine and part of the colon.
clinical.availability: Can be ordered by gastroenterologists, functional medicine doctors, and some GPs. Direct-to-consumer options exist through specialist labs. Not universally available via standard NHS/public health systems — may require private testing in many countries.

clinical.name: Glucose breath test (hydrogen and methane)
clinical.description: Uses glucose rather than lactulose as the substrate. Glucose is fully absorbed in the proximal small intestine, so this test is more specific for overgrowth in the upper small bowel but may miss distal small intestinal or colonic methanogen overgrowth. Some labs offer this as an alternative or complement to lactulose testing.
clinical.availability: Similar availability to lactulose breath testing. Less likely to detect methane elevation originating in the colon.

clinical.name: Trio-Smart breath test (hydrogen, methane, hydrogen sulphide)
clinical.description: A newer three-gas breath test that measures hydrogen, methane, and hydrogen sulphide simultaneously. Particularly useful when the clinical picture is complex or when standard two-gas tests are inconclusive. Developed in part by researchers at Cedars-Sinai.
clinical.availability: Available through the Trio-Smart lab (US-based) and select integrative practitioners internationally. Postal kit available.

clinical.name: Small intestinal aspirate and culture
clinical.description: An endoscopic procedure in which fluid from the proximal small intestine is aspirated and cultured. Considered the historical gold standard for SIBO but has significant practical limitations: it is invasive, expensive, samples only the proximal bowel, and methanogen culture requires specific anaerobic conditions not widely available. Rarely performed in routine clinical practice for IMO specifically.
clinical.availability: Specialist gastroenterology centres only. Not commonly used for IMO diagnosis.

clinical.name: Comprehensive stool analysis (PCR-based microbiome testing)
clinical.description: Advanced stool testing (e.g. from labs such as Genova Diagnostics or Doctor's Data) can detect Methanobrevibacter smithii and assess overall microbiome composition. Does not directly diagnose IMO but may support the clinical picture and reveal co-existing dysbiosis.
clinical.availability: Available through functional medicine practitioners and some private labs. Not widely used in conventional gastroenterology for this indication.

clinical.name: Thyroid function panel (TSH, Free T3, Free T4)
clinical.description: Hypothyroidism is a common driver of gut dysmotility and can cause or perpetuate methanogen overgrowth. Testing thyroid function is important in anyone with treatment-resistant or recurrent IMO, particularly when fatigue and constipation are prominent.
clinical.availability: Widely available through GP or primary care. May need to request Free T3 specifically as some standard panels only measure TSH.

related_conditions.name: IBS-C (constipation-predominant IBS)
related_conditions.explanation: Many people diagnosed with IBS-C have undetected methane overgrowth as an underlying driver. The symptom overlap is near-complete; breath testing is the primary way to distinguish them. Some researchers argue IMO is a detectable biological subtype within what is broadly labelled IBS-C.

related_conditions.name: SIBO (general)
related_conditions.explanation: IMO is classified under the broader SIBO umbrella but is mechanistically distinct: methanogens are archaea, not bacteria, and consume hydrogen produced by other microbes, making the ecosystem interdependent. Treatment approaches differ meaningfully from hydrogen-dominant SIBO.

related_conditions.name: Hydrogen SIBO
related_conditions.explanation: Hydrogen-producing bacteria supply the substrate that methanogens require to produce methane. The two conditions can co-exist (mixed SIBO), and hydrogen SIBO may precede or drive methane overgrowth.

related_conditions.name: Hydrogen sulphide SIBO
related_conditions.explanation: H2S-producing bacteria and methanogens can co-exist in the gut; three-gas breath testing is needed to identify all three gas types. Symptoms may overlap, but H2S SIBO is more associated with diarrhea and urgency.

related_conditions.name: Gastroparesis
related_conditions.explanation: Delayed gastric emptying slows the delivery of food through the GI tract, creating conditions that favour methanogen overgrowth. In people with recurrent or treatment-resistant IMO, gastroparesis should be investigated as a root cause.

related_conditions.name: Hypothyroidism
related_conditions.explanation: Low thyroid function reduces gut motility systemically, creating a slow-transit environment that favours methanogens. Correcting hypothyroidism can improve or resolve IMO in some individuals.

related_conditions.name: Gut dysbiosis
related_conditions.explanation: IMO represents a specific form of dysbiosis. Broader microbiome imbalance — including insufficient diversity and reduced butyrate-producing bacteria — often co-exists with methanogen overgrowth.

related_conditions.name: Surgical adhesions
related_conditions.explanation: Adhesions from prior abdominal surgery can cause partial obstruction or slow transit, providing conditions that promote methanogen overgrowth. IMO may be difficult to treat without addressing the adhesion-related motility problem.

related_conditions.name: Pelvic floor dysfunction
related_conditions.explanation: Pelvic floor dyssynergia (failure of pelvic floor muscles to relax during defecation) can compound constipation in IMO. The two conditions frequently co-exist and both need to be addressed for meaningful improvement.

related_conditions.name: Endometriosis
related_conditions.explanation: Endometriosis can affect bowel motility and structure, and is associated with increased rates of SIBO/IMO. Hormonal fluctuations and pelvic inflammation may contribute to both conditions.

related_conditions.name: Hypermobile EDS / HSD
related_conditions.explanation: Connective tissue laxity in hEDS can affect gut motility and structural integrity, predisposing to SIBO and IMO. GI symptoms are among the most common complaints in this population.

related_conditions.name: POTS / dysautonomia
related_conditions.explanation: Autonomic dysfunction affects gut motility via the enteric nervous system, and SIBO/IMO is a documented comorbidity of POTS. Slow transit driven by dysautonomia can perpetuate methanogen overgrowth.

related_conditions.name: Functional dyspepsia
related_conditions.explanation: Upper GI dysmotility and delayed gastric emptying, common in functional dyspepsia, can coexist with IMO and contribute to a slow-transit environment throughout the gut.

related_conditions.name: Fructose malabsorption
related_conditions.explanation: Unabsorbed fructose reaching the colon provides fermentable substrate that can exacerbate methane production. Many people with IMO find fructose-rich foods significantly worsen bloating and constipation.

related_conditions.name: Lactase deficiency
related_conditions.explanation: Undigested lactose provides fermentable substrate that can worsen symptoms in people with IMO, particularly bloating and gas.

related_conditions.name: Central sensitisation / visceral hypersensitivity
related_conditions.explanation: Chronic gut dysfunction can sensitise the visceral nervous system, leading to disproportionate pain or discomfort even when gas levels or distension are modest. Addressing IMO alone may not fully resolve pain in individuals who have developed central sensitisation.

strategies.name: Rifaximin + neomycin combination antibiotic therapy
strategies.description: The most studied antibiotic protocol for methane overgrowth. Rifaximin alone (which is effective for hydrogen SIBO) has limited activity against methanogens; adding neomycin or metronidazole significantly improves methane clearance. Typically prescribed for 10–14 days. Requires a prescribing physician.
strategies.notes: Metronidazole is sometimes used instead of neomycin and may have fewer renal concerns for those with kidney issues. Relapse is common without addressing underlying motility drivers. Not appropriate for self-prescription.

strategies.name: Allicin (stabilised allicin from garlic)
strategies.description: High-dose stabilised allicin (e.g. Allimed or equivalent) has antimicrobial activity against methanogens and is used as an alternative or adjunct to antibiotics, particularly in herbal antimicrobial protocols. Often combined with other herbal agents such as berberine or oregano oil. Protocols typically run 4–8 weeks.
strategies.notes: Evidence is largely from practitioner clinical experience and a small number of comparative studies, not large RCTs. May be a preferred option for those wanting to avoid antibiotics or for whom rifaximin is inaccessible. Discuss with a knowledgeable practitioner.

strategies.name: Low-FODMAP diet
strategies.description: Reducing fermentable carbohydrates (FODMAPs) limits the substrate available to methanogens, which can reduce gas production and bloating. A low-FODMAP diet can help manage symptoms during and after treatment but is not a cure — it starves the methanogens rather than eliminating them.
strategies.notes: Long-term strict low-FODMAP eating is not recommended as it can reduce microbial diversity. Ideally used as a short-to-medium term tool under the guidance of a FODMAP-trained dietitian, followed by a structured reintroduction phase.

strategies.name: Low-fermentation diet (elemental or semi-elemental)
strategies.description: An elemental diet — in which all nutrition is provided in pre-digested, readily absorbed form — starves methanogens of fermentable substrate. Research (including work by Dr Mark Pimentel's team at Cedars-Sinai) has shown elemental diet can normalise breath tests in a subset of SIBO/IMO cases after 2–3 weeks. A semi-elemental approach (very low-residue, easily digested foods) is a less intensive version.
strategies.notes: Full elemental diet is nutritionally challenging and socially restrictive; compliance is a significant barrier. Best considered under medical supervision, particularly for those for whom antibiotics are not tolerated or accessible.

strategies.name: Prokinetics (motility agents)
strategies.description: Agents that stimulate the migrating motor complex (MMC) — the gut's housekeeping wave between meals — help prevent reaccumulation of methanogens. Options include low-dose naltrexone (LDN), prucalopride, erythromycin (low-dose), and herbal prokinetics such as ginger, 5-HTP, or iberogast. Prokinetics are considered essential for relapse prevention.
strategies.notes: Prokinetics are often under-used in standard care. They should be continued after antimicrobial treatment — not just taken during it. Prescription options require a doctor; herbal options may be self-initiated but should be discussed with a practitioner.

strategies.name: Addressing root causes (hypothyroidism, adhesions, gastroparesis)
strategies.description: When IMO is secondary to a structural or systemic cause — hypothyroidism, surgical adhesions, diabetic neuropathy, or gastroparesis — treating that root cause is essential for durable improvement. Antimicrobials alone will produce only temporary benefit if slow transit is ongoing.
strategies.notes: Root cause investigation is often neglected in standard gastroenterology care. Functional medicine practitioners and integrative GI specialists are more likely to systematically investigate drivers.

strategies.name: Spacing meals (intermittent fasting between meals)
strategies.description: The MMC — the intestinal cleaning wave that sweeps bacteria and archaea toward the colon — only activates when the gut is in a fasted state. Eating frequently suppresses MMC activity. Allowing 4–5 hours between meals (no snacking) can support MMC function and may reduce methane accumulation over time.
strategies.notes: This does not mean prolonged fasting — just avoiding constant grazing. This lifestyle change is low-risk and can be started independently.

strategies.name: Targeted probiotic use (with care)
strategies.description: Evidence for probiotics in IMO is mixed. Certain spore-based or soil-based probiotics (e.g. Bacillus coagulans, Bacillus subtilis) and saccharomyces boulardii may support the broader microbial environment without worsening methane. Lactobacillus-heavy probiotics are sometimes reported to worsen symptoms in some individuals with SIBO/IMO.
strategies.notes: Probiotic use in SIBO/IMO is controversial and individual responses vary widely. Not universally recommended without professional input. Some practitioners suggest delaying probiotics until after antimicrobial treatment.

strategies.name: Stress management and vagal tone support
strategies.description: The vagus nerve regulates gut motility; chronic stress impairs vagal tone and can slow gut transit, creating conditions that favour methanogens. Practices such as diaphragmatic breathing, slow-paced breathing, meditation, and cold exposure may support vagal tone and indirectly improve gut motility.
strategies.notes: Unlikely to resolve IMO on its own but may support other interventions and reduce symptom severity.

things_to_avoid: High-FODMAP foods during active overgrowth — these provide fermentable substrate for methanogens and worsen gas and bloating
Frequent snacking and grazing — this suppresses the migrating motor complex and promotes ongoing methane accumulation
Excess dietary fibre (especially fermentable fibres like inulin and FOS) before methane overgrowth is treated — fibre feeds methanogens and worsens constipation in this context
Rifaximin alone without a second agent targeting methanogens — rifaximin has limited efficacy against archaea and is unlikely to clear methane overgrowth on its own
Long-term use of stimulant laxatives without addressing the underlying overgrowth — these provide temporary symptom relief but do not treat the cause and can lead to dependency
Assuming treatment is complete after one course of antibiotics — methane overgrowth has high relapse rates without ongoing motility support and root cause management
High-dose Lactobacillus probiotics during active IMO — these may worsen SIBO-related symptoms in some individuals; evidence is mixed but worth monitoring closely

uncomfortable_truths.truth: A normal colonoscopy does not rule out methane overgrowth. Most people with IMO have entirely normal colonoscopy and imaging results — these tests are not designed to detect methanogen populations.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: People often feel relief when a colonoscopy is normal and assume this means nothing is wrong. Clinicians may also incorrectly reassure patients based on normal structural findings, discouraging further investigation.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who pursue a lactulose or glucose breath test — specifically one that measures methane — often get a clear positive result that explains years of unresolved constipation and bloating, and opens the door to targeted treatment.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Adding more fibre often makes methane SIBO significantly worse. Standard advice for constipation (eat more fibre, take psyllium) can actively worsen bloating and constipation when methanogens are the cause.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: High-fibre diets for constipation are so deeply embedded in medical guidance that questioning this feels counterintuitive. Many people spend years following this advice and blame themselves for not responding.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Identifying that a low-fermentation or low-FODMAP approach produces symptom relief — while high-fibre intake worsens symptoms — is often one of the first real clues that methane overgrowth is involved.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Methane overgrowth commonly returns after treatment if the underlying motility problem is not corrected. Antibiotics are not a permanent cure — they reduce the microbial load, but if transit is still slow, methanogens repopulate.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: After a successful course of treatment, people understandably hope the problem is resolved. Accepting that ongoing management — including prokinetics, dietary habits, and possibly investigating structural causes — is necessary can feel discouraging.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who consistently use post-treatment prokinetics, maintain meal spacing, and address root causes (such as hypothyroidism or gastroparesis where applicable) report substantially longer symptom-free periods than those who rely on antibiotic courses alone.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Methane overgrowth may be increasing caloric extraction from food, contributing to weight gain that resists standard dietary interventions. Treating the overgrowth — not eating less — may be what is needed.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Weight difficulties carry significant stigma, and the idea that a gut microorganism is driving weight gain challenges deeply held beliefs about personal responsibility and dietary control.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Some individuals report easier weight management following successful treatment of IMO, consistent with research showing that methane producers enhance caloric harvest from fermented carbohydrates.

key_levers.lever: Accurate breath testing for methane (not just hydrogen)
key_levers.why_it_matters: Many breath tests only measure hydrogen. Without specifically measuring methane, the diagnosis is invisible — and all downstream treatment decisions are based on an incomplete picture.
key_levers.how_to_start: Request or order a breath test that measures both hydrogen AND methane (and ideally hydrogen sulphide via Trio-Smart). Confirm the methodology before testing.
key_levers.timeline: Testing takes a single day; results typically available within 1–2 weeks.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Rifaximin alone has limited activity against archaea. Adding neomycin, metronidazole, or high-dose allicin meaningfully improves methane clearance compared to single-agent therapy.
key_levers.how_to_start: Present breath test results to a gastroenterologist or functional medicine doctor who is familiar with IMO protocols, and discuss combination treatment options.
key_levers.timeline: A standard antibiotic course is 10–14 days; herbal protocols run 4–8 weeks. Breath test retesting 2–4 weeks post-treatment helps confirm clearance.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Without ongoing motility support after antimicrobial treatment, methanogens repopulate in slow-transit environments. Prokinetics are the primary relapse-prevention strategy.
key_levers.how_to_start: Discuss prokinetic options with a practitioner after completing antimicrobial treatment. Herbal options (ginger, iberogast) can be explored independently; prescription options (prucalopride, low-dose naltrexone) require a prescriber.
key_levers.timeline: Prokinetics are often used ongoing, not as a time-limited course. Some people discontinue after 3–6 months of symptom stability; others continue long-term.

key_levers.lever: Meal spacing (4–5 hours between meals, no snacking)
key_levers.why_it_matters: The migrating motor complex — which clears microorganisms from the small intestine — only activates in a fasted state. Constant eating suppresses this mechanism and allows methanogens to accumulate.
key_levers.how_to_start: Begin eating 3 structured meals per day with no snacks between them. Allow at least 4 hours between meals. This requires no professional guidance and can start immediately.
key_levers.timeline: Some people notice reduced bloating within days to weeks of consistent meal spacing.

key_levers.lever: Investigation and treatment of root cause
key_levers.why_it_matters: In people with recurrent or treatment-resistant IMO, a structural or systemic driver — hypothyroidism, gastroparesis, adhesions, dysautonomia — is commonly present. Treating the archaea without addressing the cause produces only temporary relief.
key_levers.how_to_start: Request thyroid function testing (including Free T3), discuss gastric emptying study if relevant, and review surgical or medical history for potential contributors to slow transit.
key_levers.timeline: Root cause investigation may take weeks to months; treatment effects (e.g. thyroid correction) may take 3–6 months to improve gut motility.

common_mistakes: Using rifaximin alone without a second agent — this is unlikely to clear methane overgrowth and may give a false sense of having treated the condition
Retesting too soon after treatment — breath testing within 2 weeks of completing antibiotics may give a false negative as the microbial population is still recovering
Treating IMO repeatedly with antibiotics without investigating why it keeps returning — this cycles through symptom relief and relapse without addressing the driver
Increasing dietary fibre to manage constipation — in the context of active methane overgrowth, this feeds the methanogens and typically worsens both bloating and constipation
Assuming the condition is resolved after symptoms improve — methanogens can be present at lower levels without full symptom resolution, and incomplete treatment leads to quicker relapse
Neglecting prokinetics after treatment — this is the most common single reason for rapid relapse
Stopping investigation after a negative hydrogen-only breath test — a test that does not measure methane cannot rule out IMO

specialists.role: Diagnosis via breath testing, prescription of combination antibiotic protocols (rifaximin + neomycin/metronidazole), investigation of structural causes (gastroparesis, adhesions), and exclusion of other diagnoses
specialists.notes: Not all gastroenterologists are familiar with IMO or routinely test for methane. Seeking one with explicit experience in SIBO/IMO, or affiliated with motility disorders programmes, significantly improves outcomes. Mainstream GIs may manage constipation symptomatically without investigating methane.

specialists.role: Often more likely to order comprehensive breath testing (including methane), investigate root causes systemically (thyroid, dysautonomia, microbiome), and use integrative treatment protocols including herbal antimicrobials and prokinetics
specialists.notes: Quality varies significantly; seek practitioners with specific GI training or credentials. Often the most accessible route to methane breath testing in regions where GIs are hesitant to test.

specialists.role: Expert guidance on dietary modification — low-FODMAP implementation, low-fermentation approaches, and structured reintroduction after treatment. Can also identify dietary patterns that may be perpetuating symptoms.
specialists.notes: Dietary intervention alone cannot resolve IMO but is an important adjunct. A dietitian with specific SIBO experience (not just general gut health) is preferable.

specialists.role: Can supervise herbal antimicrobial protocols (allicin-based), order or interpret microbiome testing, and manage prokinetic supplementation
specialists.notes: Particularly relevant for those who cannot access or prefer not to use prescription antibiotics. Evidence base for herbal protocols is growing but remains less robust than pharmaceutical options.

specialists.type: Endocrinologist or GP (for thyroid and metabolic investigation)
specialists.role: Investigation of hypothyroidism and other metabolic drivers of gut dysmotility that may underlie recurrent IMO
specialists.notes: Often the first step if weight gain, fatigue, and constipation suggest a systemic rather than purely GI cause. TSH alone may miss subclinical hypothyroidism — requesting Free T3 and Free T4 gives a fuller picture.

patient_communities.name: r/SIBO
patient_communities.notes: The largest active online community for SIBO and IMO. Contains extensive discussion of methane-specific protocols, breath test interpretation, treatment experiences, and practitioner recommendations. Highly practical for self-directed researchers.

patient_communities.name: SIBO SOS Community
patient_communities.notes: Practitioner-connected community with access to webinars, practitioner directories, and patient support groups. Includes IMO-specific content from clinicians such as Dr Allison Siebecker.

patient_communities.name: The IBS Network
patient_communities.notes: UK-based charity and patient network covering IBS and related functional GI conditions, including access to a nurse helpline and patient forums.

patient_communities.name: SIBO / IMO Support Group
patient_communities.notes: Multiple active Facebook groups exist for SIBO and IMO support. Searching 'SIBO support' or 'methane SIBO' within Facebook will surface the largest active groups. Useful for real-world treatment experience and practitioner recommendations.

learning_resources.title: Allison Siebecker's SIBO website (SIBOinfo.com)
learning_resources.description: One of the most comprehensive freely available clinical resources on SIBO and IMO. Covers methane-specific protocols, herbal and pharmaceutical treatment options, dietary approaches, and relapse prevention. Used by both patients and clinicians.

learning_resources.title: The Microbiome Connection
learning_resources.author_or_source: Dr Mark Pimentel and Dr Ami Bhatt
learning_resources.description: Written by leading SIBO/IMO researcher Dr Mark Pimentel (Cedars-Sinai), this book explains the science behind SIBO, methane overgrowth, and IBS with unusual clarity. Covers diagnosis, treatment, and the low-fermentation diet in accessible language.

learning_resources.title: Cedars-Sinai MAST Program resources
learning_resources.description: Research and clinical resources from one of the leading centres globally for SIBO and IMO research. Includes information on the Trio-Smart breath test and the low-fermentation diet developed from this programme.

learning_resources.title: SIBO SOS Summit recordings
learning_resources.description: A series of practitioner interviews and patient education sessions covering SIBO and IMO in depth. Good for understanding the range of clinical approaches and hearing from multiple practitioners on methane-specific treatment.

learning_resources.title: Intestinal Methanogen Overgrowth (IMO) — review by Pimentel et al.
learning_resources.author_or_source: Pimentel M et al. — American Journal of Gastroenterology
learning_resources.description: The paper that proposed renaming methane SIBO as 'Intestinal Methanogen Overgrowth' and outlined the evidence for its distinct biology and clinical significance. Useful for anyone wanting to understand the scientific rationale behind the IMO designation.

learning_resources.title: Dr Nirala Jacobi's SIBO Made Simple podcast
learning_resources.description: A practitioner-led podcast covering SIBO and IMO with accessible explanations of testing, treatment, and root cause investigation. Several episodes specifically address methane overgrowth and treatment protocols.

sections.relevance_signal: Chronic constipation, bloating, and slow gut motility that persists despite dietary changes — especially when a lactulose or glucose breath test shows elevated methane gas
